description: A dark jaeger was first spotted by Brandon Percival. When I arrived he and Margie Joy were watching it in scopes far to the west of South Shore Marina.  It eventually flew, a lazy flight across the reservoir and back and forth near the North Shore Picnic area.  We drove there for a closer view.  The bird was never close but on one pass it was photographed.  Photos, see below, showed a cold appearance of dark brown overall. The upper wing tips had two white outer primary feather shafts and a gray-brown collar that stood out. The head seemed small although without another jeager for comparison this was more of a thought than a positive I.D. character.  Later, from the end of Hobbie Cat Road the bird was observed flying back and forth along the low cliffs of the south shore.  There the small, rounded tails about equal to the length of the bill could be seen. Another  morphology looked for and at was the area on the belly that protruded farthest towards the water which was the lower breast area. The bird was later photographed by Glenn Walbek and others that showed the bill length and size but I was never close enough to comment on the bill.  Based on the excellent drawings and photos in Skuas and Jaegers monograph, it was apparent to me the bird was an intermediate morph juvenile.  The two light shafts on the outer primaries are the best field mark for the time I was watching the bird along with its quite lazy, bouant (sp) flight.
